extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out implies not sent) holds on all reachable executions.

Reachability analysis of the invariant "not (opted_out and sent)":
- Initial state: opted_out=false, sent=false → invariant holds.
- sent becomes true ONLY via [t2] (on send_attempt if not opted_out: sent := true). Its guard requires opted_out=false, so the resulting state has opted_out=false, and the invariant (opted_out implies not sent) is satisfied.
- opted_out becomes true ONLY via [t1] (on optout: opted_out := true, sent := false). This update atomically sets sent:=false in the SAME transition, so the moment opted_out turns true, sent is false. Invariant preserved.
- Once opted_out is true, it never returns to false (no transition assigns opted_out:=false). While opted_out is true, the only transitions touching sent are [t3] (send_attempt: sent:=false) and [t4] (tick: sent:=false); neither can make sent true. [t2] is disabled because its guard not opted_out fails. So sent stays false whenever opted_out is true.

Each event applies a single atomic update, so there is no interleaving by which sent=true and opted_out=true can coexist. The invariant is inductive and holds in every reachable state, hence the temporal property G(opted_out -> not sent) holds for all executions (trivially within the 5-event bound). No counterexample exists.
